text: The surgeon performs a transvaginal rectocele repair, reinforcing the repair with biological mesh, visible within the surgical field.

Complementary tests to support physical examination findings will confirm the real cause of symptoms and discard other associated disorders such as enterocel, colpocele or rectal interception. Physical exam should determine the level, length and width of the rectocele and discard the presence of associated pelvic organ prolapse. Patients with obstructive defecation syndrome and rectocele are further investigated with dynamic MR defecography to assess pelvic floor motion and other structural abnormalities. Patients are unable to expel the contrast retained in the rectocele during evacuation, as can be observed in these two examples.

The patient is positioned in lithotomy, and the surgical field is prepared using a lone star retractor for exposure. A solution of saline and adrenaline is injected along the posterior vaginal wall and rectocele to minimize bleeding and aid in identifying the dissection plane.

The surgeon makes a transverse vaginal mucosal incision above the introitus and dissects the rectovaginal space, separating the rectocele from the posterior vaginal wall. Digital rectal examination is used to guide the dissection and identify the correct plane. Blunt dissection is performed through an avascular space, extending up to the pouch of Douglas and laterally to the pubertalis muscle, achieving complete exposure of the rectovaginal space.

The surgeon performs midline longitudinal plication of the rectal wall using absorbable sutures, focusing on the muscular layer without entering the rectal lumen. The sutures are extended distally to the anal-rectal junction to prevent distal recurrence of the rectocele.
Levatoplasty is not routinely performed due to the risk of post-operative dyspareumia.

The surgeon reinforces the rectovaginal space by placing a cross-linked acellular porcine dermal collagen mesh. The mesh is secured with absorbable sutures placed deeply and laterally, and additional stitches are added. Excess graft material is trimmed to ensure complete coverage and reinforcement of the rectovaginal space.

The surgeon closes the vaginal wall using absorbable intermittent sutures, ensuring the closure of the mucosal incision. An intravaginal gauze pack is placed for support, while drains are not utilized in this procedure.

In our experience, if an adequate selection of patients is made, this procedure corrects the symptomatology in eighty percent of patients. We have not observed any complications related to the mesh and the recurrence rate is six percent.
